title: Building a simple wall-mounted coat rack
subtitle: Make a five-hook coat rack from a length of pine and a row of brass hooks
excerpt: How to build a simple wall-mounted coat rack from a length of planed pine and five brass hooks. Covers cutting the back board, marking and pre-drilling the hook positions, finishing the timber, and fixing to the wall on noggins or wall plugs.
sourceNotes: Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A drill bit with a conical cutting head that opens the top of a pilot hole into a tapered seat. The screw head sits flush with the timber surface rather than standing proud.

content.text: A wall-mounted coat rack is the first proper woodwork job for a beginner. The tools are all standard; the techniques (cutting square, drilling pilots, fixing to a wall) come back in every later job. A standard five-hook rack runs 600 to 700 mm long on a pine back board.
Step 1, cut the back board

Buy a length of planed pine 120 mm wide by 18 mm thick. Cut it to 650 mm long using the tenon saw and combination square. Hold the saw at the line and start the cut with two light back strokes before the full push. Sand all four cut edges and the two faces with 120 grit on the sanding block.
Step 2, mark the hook positions

Mark a horizontal pencil line 60 mm down from the top edge of the back board. Mark the first hook position 75 mm from one end, then four more at 125 mm intervals. Five hooks across a 650 mm board sit at 75, 200, 325, 450, and 575 mm from one end. Use the combination square to drop vertical reference lines from the pencil marks.
Step 3, pre-drill the hook fixings

Each brass hook has two small fixing screws supplied. Drill a 2 mm pilot hole at each hook fixing position. Drill perpendicular to the face by sighting along the drill body from two angles before pressing the trigger. Stop the drill 1 mm before breaking through the back of the board.
Step 4, mark and drill the wall fixings

Mark two wall-fixing positions on the back board, 30 mm from each end and centred top to bottom (60 mm from the top edge). Drill a 5 mm clearance hole for a number 8 screw at each position. Open the top of each clearance hole with the
countersink bit
so the screw head sits flush.
Step 5, finish the timber

Sand the back board with 180 grit, then 240 grit. Wipe off the dust with a clean rag. Apply the chosen finish: a single coat of clear furniture wax buffed to a soft sheen, two coats of clear varnish brushed thin, or two coats of paint on a primed surface. Allow each coat to dry fully before the next.
Step 6, fit the hooks to the board
Place each brass hook over its pilot holes. Drive the supplied screws by hand with the screwdriver. Snug each screw firm without driving the head into the brass; brass dimples and dents easily.
Step 7, fix to the wall

Hold the finished board to the wall at the chosen height. Mark the two screw positions through the clearance holes onto the wall with the pencil. Scan the wall with a cable and pipe detector along the marked positions. Drill a 6 mm hole into the wall at each mark. Tap a wall plug into each hole. Hold the board up to the wall, drive a 50 mm number 8 screw through each clearance hole into the plug. Check level with the spirit level before final tightening.
Variations

An oak back board reads as a step up from pine. Hardwood needs the same pilot holes but a 2.5 mm drill rather than a 2 mm, and the wall-fixing screws should be 65 mm rather than 50 mm to bite past the harder timber. A reclaimed pine board from a skip or salvage yard makes a coat rack with character; sand the face flat and treat the back for woodworm before mounting.
Care

A finished coat rack needs no maintenance beyond a dust once a month. The hooks may need a turn of the screw after a year of use; coats pull at the hooks and the screws loosen slowly. Re-tighten and re-buff the timber if the finish dulls.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Hooks loosen within a few weeks
attrs.cause: Pilot holes were too large for the supplied screws, or the timber was a soft pine that does not hold a fine thread.
attrs.fix: Remove each hook. Dip the supplied screws in PVA glue and refit. The glue grips the timber fibres and the screw bites again.

attrs.symptom: The board sits crooked once fixed to the wall
attrs.cause: The wall fixings were not at the same height, or the wall plugs slipped in the holes.
attrs.fix: Slacken the screws, level the board with the spirit level, retighten. If the plugs spin, replace with longer wall plugs or use frame-fixing screws sized to the hole depth.

attrs.symptom: Drill bit wandered when starting the pilot holes
attrs.cause: The pilot mark was not punched first, or the drill was not held perpendicular.
attrs.fix: Mark with a pencil at the cross of two square lines, then tap a centre punch into the mark. The punch leaves a dimple that holds the drill tip.
